rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Hearing On Petition To Vacate Contractual Arbitration Award
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, June 12, 2019, Line 1, PETITIONER GEORGE WOLFF'S Notice Of Hearing On Petition To Vacate Contractual Arbitration Award Petitioner George Wolff's petition to vacate contractual arbitration award is denied. The "Arbitration of All Disputes" exhibit to the parties' Litigation Retainer Agreement may allow expanded judicial review of the merits of the arbitrator's decision. (See Cable Connection, Inc. v. DirecTV, Inc. (2008) 44 Cal.4th 1334; but see Oaktree Capital Management, LP v. Bernard (2010) 182 Cal.App.4th 60, 70-71.) Even assuming it does, however, Petitioner has failed to establish that the arbitrator committed legal error or exceeded his powers in rendering the award. The arbitrator expressly found that the retainer agreement extended only to the parties' initial consultation. The award on its face (at pg. 2) contradicts Petitioner's contention that the arbitrator "refused to look at other terms of the Litigation Retainer Agreement and construe them as a whole." Even if the applicable standard of review allowed Petitioner to challenge the arbitrator's factual finding, Petitioner has not provided the Court with the complete record of the arbitration, and therefore fails to show that the finding is unsupported by substantial evidence. (See Harshad & Nasir Corp. v. Global Sign Systems, Inc. (2017) 14 Cal.App.5th 523, 537 [substantial evidence review "requires that we review the entire record, not merely the 'isolated bits of evidence selected by the [party challenging the award]' . . . . 'The ultimate test is whether it is reasonable for a trier of fact to make the ruling in question in light of the whole record.'"].) Petitioner's contentions that "no . . . . limitations periods exist at all" as to claims brought in arbitration (Mot. at 3) and that the arbitrator lacked jurisdiction to decide the limitations issue (id. at 8) are inconsistent with Petitioner's position at the arbitration. (See id. at 5 [referring to Petitioner's "argument in his Closing Brief that Code [sic] Civil Proc. § 337 provides the only Statute of Limitations applicable [sic] a dispute as to . . . the Written Litigation Retainer Agreement."].) It is also wrong as a matter of California law. Wagner Construction Co. v. Pacific Mechanical Corp. (2007) 41 Cal.4th 19, upon which Petitioner relies, stands for the opposite proposition: "Where, as here, the parties have agreed to arbitrate any dispute arising out of their contract, the affirmative defense that the statute of limitations has run is for the arbitrator rather than the court to decide." (Id. at 23 [emphasis added].) The Court notes that both parties have incorrectly filed papers in this matter under the case number pertaining to the original petition to compel arbitration (CPF-16-515366), rather than under the case number pertaining to the instant petition to vacate the arbitration award (CPF-19-516575). To cure this mistake and avoid future confusion, the Court hereby consolidates the two actions for all purposes. Any future filings should be made under the original case number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the respondents is required to prepare a proposed order which states that the motion was not opposed and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Calendar Matter: Petitioner Lindsay Crist'S Amended Motion To Quash Subpoena Of Defendant 2 Requesting Pltf'S Information From Defendant 1, Or, In The Alternative, For A Protective Order Regarding Request For Paper And/Or Electronic Records Or Data
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, June 12, 2019, Line 2, Petitioner Lindsay Crist'S Amended Motion To Quash Subpoena Of Defendant 2 Requesting Pltf'S Information From Defendant 1, Or, In The Alternative, For A Protective Order Regarding Request For Paper And/Or Electronic Records Or Data Pro Tem Judge Peter Catalanotti, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The Petition to Quash Service is continued 30 days (July 10) to allow petitioner to have the Petition and motion served by a professional process server, per her request in Declaration of Lindsay Crist Regarding Service. Petitioner is ordered (within 5 days of this order) to have a professional process server hand serve copies of the petition and other moving papers on both AIRBNB and the Department of Fair Employment and Housing at the addresses listed in the March 22, 2019 email from AIRBNB attached to her memo of points and authorities. Petitioner shall file a new proof of service within 10 days of this order. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to pcatalanotti@fmglaw.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: JASON ROUSSOS VS. POUR GUYS, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Pour Guys Soma Llc Dba Tempest Bar & Grill'S Motion To Modify The Scope Of A Protective Order
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, June 12, 2019, Line 4, Defendant Pour Guys Soma LLC's Motion To Modify The Scope Of A Protective Order Pro Tem Judge Peter Catalanotti, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Defendants' Motion to Modify Scope of Protective Order is DENIED without prejudice. Defendants seek to modify the September 26, 2017 protective order in this case to allow disclosure of protected documents produced in this case to be produced in a companion case, CGC-19-575214 filed by defendants against two of plaintiff's treating doctors. On April 11, 2019, this Court denied defendants' Motion for Leave to File a Cross-Complaint against the two treating doctors, stating, "[defendants] may also file independent actions for indemnity and contribution following trial on the underlying claims." On April 12, 2019, defendants filed a separate action against the two treating doctors. Defendants cite to Kraszewski v. State Farm Gen. Ins. Co. (N.D. Cal. 1991) 139 F.R.D. 156 for support. In Kraszewski, the Federal Court allowed modification of the protective order after Defendant State Farm was found liable for sex discrimination. The first lawsuit and second lawsuit were similar sex discrimination claims against the same defendant. Here, there has been no finding of liability against defendants. Therefore, the reasoning in Kraszewski is inapplicable. Defendants cite to Raymond Handling Concepts Corp. v. Superior Court (1995) 39 Cal. App. 4th 584 for support. In Raymond, the Court was asked to determine whether the trial court abused its discretion by signing a protective order that allowed sharing of discovery in similar cases if counsel notified other counsel and the new counsel stipulated to being bound by the protective order. The Court found that the trial court did not abuse its discretion in entering the protective order. Here, the present action involves a stipulated protective order that contains no similar provision. Therefore, the reasoning in Raymond is inapplicable. Finally, defendants fail to state in the Motion or Reply what categories of documents defendants seek to release to opposing counsel in the companion case. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to pcatalanotti@fmglaw.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion To Declare Plaintiff A Vexatious Litigant And To Require Plaintiff To Furnish Security
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, June 12, 2019, Line 5, DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO'S Motion To Declare Plaintiff A Vexatious Litigant And To Require Plaintiff To Furnish Security Grant in part. No timely opposition filed. On June 7, 2019, Mr. Sales filed a one-page "Opposition" that does not state any intelligible ground for opposing the motion. The Court finds that Plaintiff James Sales, having within the past year commenced in propria persona six lawsuits against the City and County of San Francisco that have been finally determined adversely to him, is a vexatious litigant. (Code Civ. Proc. § 391(b)(1).) Because the Court has sustained the City's demurrer to the amended complaint in this action without leave to amend, it denies the City's request that it require Plaintiff to furnish security under Code of Civil Procedure Section 391.1, a provision which only applies to "pending" litigation "at any time until final judgment is entered." However, on the Court's own motion, the Court will enter a prefiling order which prohibits Mr. Sales from filing any new litigation in the courts of this state in propria persona without first obtaining leave of the presiding judge of the court where the litigation is proposed to be filed. (Code Civ. Proc. § 391.7(a); see Shalant v. Girardi (2011) 51 Cal.4th 1164, 1169-1171.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling adversely affects, in whole or in part, any party who is not represented by an attorney, then any other party (whether represented by an attorney or not) may submit on the tentative ruling without appearing for the hearing only if the adversely affected self-represented party has stated in writing or orally that it, he or she will not appear at the hearing. Counsel for the City is required to prepare a proposed order which states that the motion was not opposed and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: FABRICOR LLC ET AL VS. GUNEET BAJWA ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Plaintiffs Motion For Entitlement To Attorney Fees And Amount Of Attorney Fees
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, June 12, 2019, Line 6, PLAINTIFFS FABRICOR LLC, SAMUEL LEIZOREK'S Motion For Entitlement To Attorney Fees And Amount Of Attorney Fees Plaintiffs Fabricor LLC and Samuel Leizorek's motion for an order determining their entitlement to attorneys' fees is granted. Defendants Guneet Bajwa and Sushil Patel do not dispute that Fabricor and Leizorek are the parties prevailing on the Guaranty and are entitled under that agreement to an award of "any and all costs, fees and expenses (including without limitation, reasonable attorneys' fees and costs) and expenses incurred by Guaranteed Parties in enforcing any rights and remedies under the Guaranty." The Court determines that the reasonable amount of attorneys' fees to which Fabricor and Leizorek is entitled is $94,355.00, plus costs in the amount of $5,300.22. Plaintiffs are entitled to reasonable attorneys' fees incurred in bringing and prosecuting the complaint, including seeking writs of attachment (whether or not successful). However, they are not entitled to fees incurred in connection with the cross-complaint (including their demurrer, motion to strike, and an unfiled motion to quash for lack of personal jurisdiction). Those fees were not incurred "on the contract" or "in enforcing any rights and remedies under the Guaranty," but rather in connection with unrelated claims against third parties and Leizorek on the basis of other agreements, to which Plaintiffs successfully raised a misjoinder defense. Further, the majority of those fees were incurred after the Court issued its February 26, 2019 order granting Plaintiffs' motion for a right to attach order and writs of attachment, which Plaintiffs contend was the precipitating factor that led Defendants to finally make payment on the underlying obligation. The Court has calculated the amount as follows: Plaintiffs seek a total of $315,664.60, of which Defendants have shown that $145,075.00 is attributable to the proceedings relating to the cross-complaint and an additional $50,399.50 is attributable to work unrelated to the guaranty provision and attachment proceedings. (Johnson Decl. 12, 17 & Ex. A.) The Court previously awarded $25,835 in connection with its attachment order, which yields a net award of $94,355.00. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the defendants is required to prepare a proposed order which states that the motion was not opposed and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)
